meta.title: Daily Words
meta.description: A new word to learn every day. Deterministic word-of-the-day selected by calendar date.

words.definition: The pleasant, earthy smell produced when rain falls on dry soil.
words.example: After the long drought, the first storm filled the air with petrichor.
words.origin: Greek petra ‚Äėstone‚Äô + ichor, the fluid said to flow in the veins of the gods.

words.definition: Lasting for a very short time; fleeting and transient.
words.example: The beauty of the cherry blossom is ephemeral, gone within a week.

words.definition: The realization that each passer-by is living a life as vivid and complex as your own.
words.example: Standing in the crowd, a wave of sonder reminded her how many stories surrounded her.
words.origin: Coined by John Koenig in the Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows.

words.definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words.
words.example: The view from the summit gave him an ineffable sense of peace.
words.origin: Latin ineffabilis, from in- ‚Äėnot‚Äô + effabilis ‚Äėable to be uttered‚Äô.

words.definition: Denoting a period of time in the past that was idyllically happy and peaceful.
words.example: She looked back on the halcyon days of her childhood summers.
words.origin: Greek halkuŇćn, a mythical bird that calmed the seas.

words.definition: Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear.
words.example: The narrator's mellifluous voice made the audiobook a joy.

words.definition: The occurrence of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way.
words.example: Finding that book was pure serendipity ‚ÄĒ it answered a question I'd had for years.
words.origin: Coined by Horace Walpole in 1754, from the tale The Three Princes of Serendip.

words.definition: Full of or shedding light; bright or shining, especially in the dark.
words.example: The luminous dial of the watch glowed softly at night.

words.definition: A strong desire to travel and explore the world.
words.example: Her wanderlust took her across three continents in a single year.

words.definition: Exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical.
words.example: His quixotic plan to end all traffic in the city won hearts but few votes.
words.origin: From Don Quixote, the hero of Cervantes' novel.

words.definition: Cheerful and full of energy; overflowing with enthusiasm.
words.example: The ebullient host greeted every guest with a burst of laughter.

words.definition: The lowest or most unsuccessful point in a situation.
words.example: The team's nadir came after five straight defeats.

words.definition: Vivacious and enthusiastic; bubbling with liveliness.
words.example: Her effervescent personality lit up the entire room.

words.definition: The state or situation of being alone, often by choice and peacefully.
words.example: He craved the solitude of the mountains after a hectic week.

words.definition: A soft, gentle breeze.
words.example: A warm zephyr drifted across the meadow at dusk.
words.origin: Greek Zephuros, god of the west wind.

words.definition: Wicked, villainous, or criminal in nature.
words.example: The detective uncovered a nefarious scheme to defraud investors.

words.definition: A solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases.
words.example: Technology is powerful, but it is no panacea for social problems.

words.definition: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties; toughness.
words.example: The community's resilience after the flood inspired the whole nation.

words.definition: Extremely happy, peaceful, or picturesque.
words.example: They spent an idyllic afternoon by the lake.

words.definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere.
words.example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern life.

words.definition: A small waterfall, or a process whereby something happens in a series of stages.
words.example: A cascade of errors followed the single missed deadline.

words.definition: Self-confidence or assurance, especially in a demanding situation.
words.example: She handled the hostile questions with remarkable aplomb.
words.origin: French √† plomb ‚Äėaccording to the plummet, perpendicular‚Äô.

words.definition: Green with grass or other rich vegetation; lush.
words.example: The verdant hills rolled endlessly toward the horizon.
words.origin: Old French verdoyant, from verdoyer ‚Äėbe green‚Äô.

words.definition: A fine, filmy substance; something extremely light, thin, and delicate.
words.example: The dress was made of a gossamer so light it seemed to float.
words.origin: Middle English, probably ‚Äėgoose summer‚Äô, a mild period when geese were eaten.

words.definition: Emitting light as a result of being heated; full of strong emotion; brilliant.
words.example: The critic gave the performance an incandescent review.

words.definition: To follow a winding course; to wander aimlessly and idly.
words.example: We let the conversation meander across a dozen topics.
words.origin: From the Maiandros, a notably winding river in ancient Turkey.

words.definition: Of such excellence, grandeur, or beauty as to inspire great admiration or awe.
words.example: The choir reached a sublime crescendo that silenced the hall.

words.definition: The quality of being determined and persistent.
words.example: It was sheer tenacity, not talent, that carried her to the finish.

words.definition: A state of being pleasantly lost in one's thoughts; a daydream.
words.example: The gentle hum of the train lulled him into a reverie.

words.definition: To light or set alight; to arouse or inspire an emotion.
words.example: A single question can kindle a lifelong curiosity.
words.origin: Old Norse kynda ‚Äėto set fire to‚Äô.

words.definition: Expressed clearly; easy to understand; mentally sharp.
words.example: Her lucid explanation made the hard concept feel simple.

words.definition: An abundant supply of good things; a horn of plenty.
words.example: The market was a cornucopia of colours, spices, and sounds.

words.definition: Beautiful or creative; divinely inspired; peaceful and perfect.
words.example: The garden had an almost elysian calm at first light.
words.origin: From Elysium, the paradise of Greek mythology.

words.definition: To understand after much thought; to penetrate the meaning of.
words.example: She could not fathom why the plan had failed so suddenly.
words.origin: Old English f√¶thm ‚Äėoutstretched arms‚Äô, later a unit of depth.

words.definition: Showing luminous colours that seem to change when seen from different angles.
words.example: The beetle's iridescent shell shimmered green and gold.

words.definition: Comfort or consolation in a time of distress or sadness.
words.example: He found solace in long walks along the shore.

words.definition: Producing powerful, clear images in the mind; intensely bright.
words.example: She had a vivid memory of the day they first met.

words.definition: Playfully quaint or fanciful, especially in an appealing way.
words.example: The shop was full of whimsical clocks that ran backwards.

words.definition: The time at which something is most powerful or successful; the highest point.
words.example: The empire reached its zenith in the second century.

words.definition: To charm or enchant, sometimes in a deceptive way.
words.example: The storyteller beguiled the children for hours.

words.definition: A modulation or rhythm in sound; the flow or beat of speech or movement.
words.example: The poem's gentle cadence made it easy to remember.

words.definition: Light, delicate, and translucent; almost transparent.
words.example: Diaphanous curtains let the morning light pour in.

words.definition: A sudden, striking realization or moment of insight.
words.example: In the shower she had an epiphany about how to fix the bug.

words.definition: Very attentive to accuracy and detail; hard to please.
words.example: A fastidious editor, she caught every stray comma.

words.definition: To shock or excite someone into taking decisive action.
words.example: The speech galvanized the volunteers into action overnight.
words.origin: From Luigi Galvani, who studied electrical effects on muscles.

words.definition: The night of 31 October, traditionally associated with ghosts and costumes.
words.example: The children carved lanterns for Halloween.

words.definition: Showing a casual lack of concern; carefree and unbothered.
words.example: He gave an insouciant shrug and carried on whistling.

words.definition: To place two things close together for contrasting effect.
words.example: The film juxtaposes quiet villages with roaring cities.

words.definition: A constantly changing pattern or sequence of elements.
words.example: The festival was a kaleidoscope of music, colour, and food.

words.definition: A complicated, irregular network of passages; an intricate maze.
words.example: The old town was a labyrinth of narrow, twisting lanes.
words.origin: Greek labyrinthos, the maze of the Minotaur.

words.definition: A short musical composition, typically for piano, evoking the night.
words.example: She played a gentle nocturne as the room fell dark.
words.origin: French, from Latin nocturnus ‚Äėof the night‚Äô.
